conversation.title: Meeting Someone for the First Time

lines.speaker: Ana
lines.en: Hello, good morning.

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.en: Good morning.

lines.speaker: Ana
lines.en: My name is Ana. What's your name?

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.en: My name is Carlos. Nice to meet you.

lines.speaker: Ana
lines.en: Nice to meet you, Carlos.

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.en: How are you?

lines.speaker: Ana
lines.en: Very well, thank you. And you?

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.en: Good, thanks.

lines.speaker: Ana
lines.en: Pleased to meet you.

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.en: Likewise.

usefulExpressions.title: Useful Expressions for Greetings and Introductions

expressions.description: Ser means "to be" and is used to describe identity, origin, profession, and characteristics.
expressions.note: When introducing yourself, soy is often used instead of me llamo.

expressions.description: Means "pleased to meet you" or "delighted." Encantado is used by a man; Encantada by a woman.
expressions.note: Often used after learning someone's name.

expressions.description: A reflexive verb meaning "to find oneself" or, in greetings, "to be."
expressions.note: Less common than ¿Cómo estás?, but useful for asking about someone's condition.

expressions.description: Means "to be called" and is the most common way to ask and say someone's name.
Means "right?" or "isn't that true?" Used to confirm information.
expressions.note: Useful when meeting someone and checking information.

expressions.description: Used to talk about where someone is from.
expressions.note: A very common topic during introductions.

examples.es: No soy de Chile, sino de Perú.
examples.en: I'm not from Chile, but rather from Peru.

expressions.note: Useful when correcting information about yourself.

expressions.description: Means "what." Used to ask for information.
expressions.note: Common in conversation after introductions.

expressions.description: Means "who." Used to ask about a person's identity.
expressions.note: Useful when introducing other people.

examples.en: How long have you lived here?
How long have you been studying Spanish?

expressions.note: A common follow-up question after introductions.

expressions.description: Has several meanings, including "already," "now," or "I understand."
expressions.note: Frequently used in conversation when getting to know someone.
Used to pause, think, or invite someone to explain something.
Used to react to information someone has shared.
Often used to start, continue, or end a conversation politely.

expressions.description: Literally means "it seems like a lie," but is used like "it's hard to believe."

examples.es: ¡Parece mentira que ya hayan pasado diez años!
examples.en: It's hard to believe ten years have already passed!

examples.es: Parece mentira que nos conozcamos por fin.
examples.en: It's hard to believe we're finally meeting.

expressions.note: Can be used when meeting someone after a long time or after only knowing them online.

quickReference.example: No soy de Chile, sino de Perú.

lines.speaker: Sofía
lines.es: Hola, Diego. ¿Qué te gusta hacer en tu tiempo libre?
lines.en: Hello, Diego. What do you like to do in your free time?

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.es: Me gusta hacer senderismo y viajar. ¿Y a ti?
lines.en: I like hiking and traveling. And you?

lines.speaker: Sofía
lines.es: Me gusta leer y escuchar música.
lines.en: I like reading and listening to music.

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.en: What kind of music do you like?

lines.speaker: Sofía
lines.es: Me gusta el jazz y el rock.
lines.en: I like jazz and rock.

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.es: A mí también me gusta el jazz.
lines.en: I like jazz too.

lines.speaker: Sofía
lines.en: What do you think of classical music?

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.es: Para serte sincero, me gusta bastante.
lines.en: To be honest, I like it quite a bit.

lines.speaker: Sofía
lines.es: A mí no me gusta mucho.
lines.en: I don't like it very much.

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.es: De hecho, la escucho desde hace años.
lines.en: In fact, I've been listening to it for years.

lines.speaker: Sofía
lines.en: Well, we all have different tastes.

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.en: I agree.

lines.speaker: Sofía
lines.es: A propósito, ¿qué te parece esta nueva película?
lines.en: By the way, what do you think of this new movie?

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.es: Creo que es interesante, aunque un poco larga.
lines.en: I think it's interesting, although a bit long.

lines.speaker: Sofía
lines.es: Yo también. Por otra parte, los actores son excelentes.
lines.en: Me too. On the other hand, the actors are excellent.

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.en: Yes, I support that opinion.

lines.speaker: Sofía
lines.es: Quisiera verla otra vez este fin de semana.
lines.en: I'd like to watch it again this weekend.

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.en: Perfect! I hope we can go together.

phrases.es: No me gustan las películas de terror.

phrases.en: What do you think about that?
I maintain that it is correct.

phrases.es: Me gusta más el café que el té.
phrases.en: I like coffee more than tea.
I would like to learn Italian.
I would like to visit Spain someday.

phrases.es: Me gusta viajar, ya sea en tren o en avión.
phrases.en: I like traveling, whether by train or by plane.

phrases.es: Para serte sincero, prefiero quedarme en casa.
phrases.en: To be honest, I prefer staying home.

usefulExpressions.title: Useful Expressions for Likes and Opinions

expressions.description: Gustar means "to be pleasing to" and is used to talk about likes. The verb agrees with the thing being liked, not the person.
Means "to love." Works the same way as gustar.
Means "to interest." Works the same way as gustar.
Means "to fascinate." Works the same way as gustar.
Means "to bother." Works the same way as gustar.
Means "to matter." Works the same way as gustar.

examples.en: Do you mind if I open the window?

expressions.description: Means "to bore." Works the same way as gustar.
Means "to hope that" or "to expect that."

examples.es: Espero que la película sea buena.
examples.en: I hope the movie is good.

expressions.note: Often used when recommending something.

examples.es: Me gusta cocinar como mi abuela.
examples.en: I like cooking like my grandmother.

examples.es: Me gusta el fútbol desde niño.
examples.en: I've liked soccer since I was a child.

examples.es: Escucho jazz desde hace cinco años.
examples.en: I've been listening to jazz for five years.

expressions.note: Used to describe how long you've liked something.

examples.es: Me gusta viajar, ya sea en tren o en avión.
examples.en: I like traveling whether by train or plane.

examples.es: Disfruto la música, ya sea clásica o moderna.
examples.en: I enjoy music whether classical or modern.

expressions.description: Means "that is," "in other words," or "I mean."

examples.es: Me encanta leer; o sea, leo todos los días.
examples.en: I love reading; I mean, I read every day.

examples.es: No me gusta mucho; o sea, está bien.
examples.en: I don't like it very much; I mean, it's okay.

expressions.note: Very common in spoken Spanish.

examples.es: Me gusta mucho el cine. De hecho, voy todas las semanas.
examples.en: I really like movies. In fact, I go every week.

examples.es: De hecho, es mi libro favorito.
examples.en: In fact, it's my favorite book.

expressions.note: Used to add supporting information.

examples.es: A propósito, ¿te gusta la música latina?
examples.en: By the way, do you like Latin music?

examples.es: A propósito, ¿qué opinas de esa película?
examples.en: By the way, what do you think of that movie?

expressions.note: Used to introduce a new topic.

examples.en: What do you think of this song?
What do you think of the idea?

expressions.note: One of the most common ways to ask for opinions.

examples.es: Para serte sincero, no me gusta mucho.
examples.en: To be honest, I don't like it very much.

examples.es: Para serte sincera, prefiero el otro.
examples.en: To be honest, I prefer the other one.

expressions.note: Used before expressing a personal opinion.

examples.es: No soporto las películas de terror.

expressions.note: Stronger than no me gusta.
Useful when expressing agreement.

expressions.description: Means "to maintain," "to argue," or "to hold an opinion."

examples.es: Sostengo que es una buena idea.
examples.en: I maintain that it's a good idea.

examples.es: Muchos sostienen que es la mejor opción.
examples.en: Many argue that it is the best option.

expressions.note: More formal than creo que.

examples.es: Me gusta vivir aquí. Por otra parte, es muy caro.
examples.en: I like living here. On the other hand, it is very expensive.

examples.es: La película es divertida. Por otra parte, es demasiado larga.
examples.en: The movie is entertaining. On the other hand, it is too long.

expressions.note: Used to present a contrasting opinion.

examples.en: I would like to learn Italian.
I would like to try that food.

expressions.note: A polite way to express wishes and preferences.

examples.es: Me parece que es una buena idea.
examples.en: It seems to me that it's a good idea.

examples.es: Me parece que no es necesario.
examples.en: It seems to me that it's not necessary.

expressions.note: A softer way to express an opinion.

examples.es: Para mí, es la mejor canción del álbum.
examples.en: For me, it's the best song on the album.

examples.es: Para mí, el verano es mejor que el invierno.
examples.en: For me, summer is better than winter.

quickReference.example: Para serte sincero, prefiero el otro.

lines.speaker: Ana
lines.en: Hi, Marta! How's it going?

lines.speaker: Marta
lines.en: Very well, thanks. And you?

lines.speaker: Ana
lines.en: Good too. What's new?

lines.speaker: Marta
lines.es: Pues, me mudé a un apartamento nuevo la semana pasada.
lines.en: Well, I moved to a new apartment last week.

lines.speaker: Ana
lines.en: Really? That's great!

lines.speaker: Marta
lines.es: Sí, y todavía estoy organizando todo.
lines.en: Yes, and I'm still organizing everything.

lines.speaker: Ana
lines.en: Tell me, what's the place like?

lines.speaker: Marta
lines.es: Es pequeño, pero muy cómodo. Incluso tiene un balcón.
lines.en: It's small, but very comfortable. It even has a balcony.

lines.speaker: Ana
lines.es: ¡Qué bien! ¿Todavía te dedicas al diseño gráfico?
lines.en: That's great! Do you still work in graphic design?

lines.speaker: Marta
lines.es: Claro. De hecho, trabajo desde casa ahora.
lines.en: Of course. In fact, I work from home now.

lines.speaker: Ana
lines.en: Wow, that sounds very convenient.

lines.speaker: Marta
lines.es: Sí, aunque a veces extraño la oficina.
lines.en: Yes, although sometimes I miss the office.

lines.speaker: Ana
lines.es: ¿Te apetece tomar un cafecito algún día?
lines.en: Would you like to have a little coffee sometime?

lines.speaker: Marta
lines.en: Of course!

lines.speaker: Ana
lines.en: Well, talk to you soon. Bye!

lines.speaker: Marta
lines.en: Bye!

phrases.en: What are you doing these days?
Look / Imagine that / You know what?

expressions.description: Means "kisses." In many Spanish-speaking countries, friends and family often greet each other with one or two cheek kisses.
expressions.note: Often used at the end of messages, emails, or conversations.

expressions.description: One of the most common ways to say "How's it going?", "How are things?", or "What's up?"
expressions.note: Less formal than ¿Cómo está usted?
A common question when reconnecting with someone.

expressions.description: A diminutive is a word ending used to make something sound smaller, friendlier, or more affectionate. Common examples: café → cafecito, casa → casita, perro → perrito, momento → momentito.

examples.en: Shall we have a little coffee?

expressions.note: The diminutive often expresses friendliness rather than actual size.

expressions.description: Means "Tell me." Used to encourage someone to continue speaking.
expressions.note: Very common in casual conversation.

expressions.description: Means "to work in" or "to dedicate oneself to." Often used when asking about someone's profession.

examples.en: What do you do for a living?

expressions.note: Very useful when getting to know someone.

examples.en: I like it, although it's expensive.
Even though I was tired, I went.

expressions.note: Used to connect contrasting ideas.

expressions.description: Means "well..." Often used to start an answer or give yourself time to think.
expressions.note: Extremely common in spoken Spanish.

expressions.description: Literally means "look" or "notice." Often used like "You know what?", "Imagine that", or "Interestingly enough."

examples.en: You know what, I lived there too.

expressions.note: Very common in conversation.
One of the most common agreement expressions.
Used to react positively to news.
Very common when talking about recent events.
A common topic when catching up with someone.
Used to emphasize additional information.
Very common informal farewells throughout the Spanish-speaking world.
